county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1912 from Chouteau County. Named for James J. Hill, Great Northern Railway builder. Contains Rocky Boy's Indian Reservation (Chippewa-Cree).
county_metadata.research_tips: Hill County Clerk and Recorder in Havre has records from 1912. Havre was a Great Northern Railway division point founded by Hill's railroad — railroad employment records identify many families. Rocky Boy's Indian Reservation (Chippewa-Cree) is partially in Hill County; BIA Rocky Boy Agency records at NARA Denver include census rolls and allotment records. Fort Assinniboine (1879-1911) military records at NARA document soldiers and later served as the reservation headquarters. Havre Beneath the Streets preserves underground business district history. Check Chouteau County for pre-1912 records. The H. Earl Clack Memorial Museum in Havre has pioneer family files. Havre's ethnic communities included Japanese railroad workers, documented in GN Railway records at the Minnesota Historical Society.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1912 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1912. County Clerk and Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1943.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Early gold mining center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County Clerk and Recorder in Boulder holds records from 1865. Jefferson County was an early placer gold mining area — mining district records from camps like Jefferson City, Wickes, Corbin, and Basin are at MHS. The Montana Developmental Center (formerly the Montana School for the Feeble-Minded, est. 1893) in Boulder has patient records (restricted access). The Jefferson Valley Museum in Whitehall has local family files. Wickes smelter and Corbin mining camp records document immigrant miners. Check territorial records at MHS for early settlement prior to organized county government. The Northern Pacific Railroad ran through the county — railroad land sale records at MHS. Boulder Hot Springs attracted health seekers whose records may appear in local hotel registers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1920 from Fergus and Cascade counties. Named for the Judith River basin.
county_metadata.research_tips: Judith Basin County Clerk and Recorder in Stanford has records from 1920. The Judith Basin was famous open-range cattle country in the 1880s — Charlie Russell painted many of his iconic western scenes here. Roundup records and cattle brand books at MHS identify pioneer stockmen. The Judith Basin County Museum in Stanford holds homesteading-era family records. Check Fergus and Cascade counties for pre-1920 records. Utica, Hobson, and other small towns were centers of the cattle industry before farming; the Utica Museum has ranching family files. The Milwaukee Road railroad brought homesteaders after 1900. BLM's GLO records document original homestead patents. Many families left during the drought and depression of the 1920s-30s — abandoned homestead files at NARA contain personal information about these settlers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1923 from Flathead and Missoula counties. Located on the Flathead (Salish-Kootenai) Indian Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lake County Clerk and Recorder in Polson has records from 1923. Lake County is largely within the Flathead Indian Reservation (Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes) — tribal enrollment records at the Flathead Agency and BIA records at NARA Denver include census rolls, allotment records, and school files (St. Ignatius Mission School, Ursuline Academy). The Miracle of America Museum in Polson has local history collections. St. Ignatius Mission (est. 1854) records at Gonzaga University and the Oregon Province Archives are among Montana's earliest church records. Check Flathead and Missoula counties for pre-1923 records. The Flathead Reservation was opened to homesteading in 1910 — land allotment and patent records at BLM document both tribal and non-tribal settlers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as Edgerton County, renamed Lewis and Clark County in 1868. Helena is the state capital. Last Chance Gulch gold strike (1864).
county_metadata.research_tips: Lewis and Clark County Clerk and Recorder in Helena holds records from 1865. Helena grew from the Last Chance Gulch gold strike (1864) and became the territorial capital in 1875 and state capital in 1889. The Montana Historical Society (MHS) in Helena is the central state repository — territorial records, governor's papers, legislative records, and the state vital records index are all here. Carroll College archives hold Catholic Diocese of Helena records. The Montana State Genealogical Society library is in Helena. Fort Harrison military records at NARA. The Helena Independent Record newspaper (1866+) is an exceptional genealogical source. Helena's wealth from gold and silver mining created a millionaire's row; probate and estate records at the district court are extensive for the Victorian era.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1920 from Chouteau and Hill counties. Hi-Line county along the Great Northern Railway.
county_metadata.research_tips: Liberty County Clerk and Recorder in Chester has records from 1920. Liberty County was created during the peak homesteading period along the Great Northern Railway Hi-Line corridor. Many settlers came from Scandinavian communities in Minnesota and the Dakotas. Check Chouteau and Hill counties for pre-1920 records. The Liberty County Museum in Chester holds homestead-era family photographs and records. BLM's GLO database documents all federal land patents in the county. Severe drought in the 1920s and 1930s caused massive population loss — abandoned homestead files at NARA contain biographical details about departing families. The Liberty County Times newspaper is on microfilm at MHS. Grain elevator records and agricultural cooperative minutes at MHS may name farming families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909 from Flathead County. Northwestern Montana timber country.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County Clerk and Recorder in Libby holds records from 1909. Lincoln County was heavily timber-dependent — lumber company records (J. Neils Lumber Co., Libby Lumber Co.) at MHS and University of Montana archives identify workers and their families. The Heritage Museum in Libby has local genealogy collections. The Kootenai River and Lake Koocanusa shaped settlement patterns. Libby vermiculite mine (W.R. Grace) operated 1919-1990; mine employment records are important for asbestos exposure claims and family research. Check Flathead County for pre-1909 records. Troy, Eureka, and other small towns have distinct settlement histories. The Tobacco Plains area near Eureka was settled by ranchers before the timber era. Great Northern Railway records at the Minnesota Historical Society document railroad workers along this corridor.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Dawson and Richland counties. Eastern Montana ranching and farming county.
county_metadata.research_tips: McCone County Clerk and Recorder in Circle holds records from 1919. McCone County was formed during the homesteading era — the homestead boom and subsequent drought-driven bust shaped the county's demographics. The McCone County Museum in Circle has pioneer family files and homestead-era photographs. Check Dawson and Richland counties for pre-1919 records. BLM's GLO database documents original homestead patents. The Circle Banner newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. Ranching families who survived the drought era are well-documented in livestock brand books at the Montana Department of Livestock. The county's sparse population means that county commissioner meeting minutes, school records, and church registers are particularly valuable for identifying early families. Some Norwegian and German-Russian immigrant families settled here.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Virginia City was the second territorial capital (1865-1875).
county_metadata.research_tips: Madison County Clerk and Recorder in Virginia City has records from 1865. Virginia City was Montana's second territorial capital (1865-1875) and a gold rush center — Alder Gulch was one of the richest placer deposits in the West. Vigilante records and miners' court proceedings from 1863-64 at MHS document the famous Plummer gang executions. The Thompson-Hickman Memorial Museum in Virginia City has family files and mining-era records. Nevada City (nearby ghost town) preserves original buildings and artifacts. The Madison Valley ranching community predates the gold rush. Check territorial records at MHS for early mining district records. Tobacco Root Mining District records at BLM and MHS. The Madisonian newspaper (1873+) in Virginia City is one of Montana's oldest continuously published newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1867 from Edgerton County (now Lewis and Clark). Named for Acting Territorial Governor Thomas Francis Meagher.
county_metadata.research_tips: Meagher County Clerk and Recorder in White Sulphur Springs holds records from 1867. White Sulphur Springs was a frontier health resort — hotel and bathhouse registers may document visitors. The Castle mining district (silver and lead) produced mining camp records at MHS. The Meagher County Museum has local family files. Meagher County was an early ranching center — the Smith River valley attracted stockmen from the 1860s. Check Lewis and Clark County for pre-1867 records. Castle was a booming mining town in the 1880s-90s that became a ghost town; mining company records and Castle newspaper at MHS. The Musselshell Valley and Smith River areas have distinct settlement patterns. Fort Logan military cantonment records (1869-80) at NARA document soldiers stationed in the area.
